EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 40.1 feet Eagle III commercial crab fishing vessel collided with the Coos Bay north jetty and capsized around 830 pm on 1/19/2016. The vessel had left Charleston, OR but was registered in Port Orford, OR. There were 4 people on board. The captain survived by making it to shore. One male body was found on the Coos Bay north jetty along with boat debris. Two people are still missing and assumed deceased. The debris has become a hazard in the Coos Bay bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained below freezing surface temperatures for the Columbia Gorge, and areas of the Willamette Valley as a couple of precipitating events moved over the area Jan 3 and Jan 4. This resulted in around an inch of snow followed by freezing rain for the lower elevations of much of Northwest Oregon.  The winter weather resulted in transportation issues and school closures. There were a lot of car accidents and minor injuries from slips and falls on the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to Two inches of snow fell Jan 3rd followed by 0.10 to 0.20 inch of freezing rain...resulting in very slick driving conditions through Jan 4th. The local news reported several car accidents due to the frozen precipitation on the roads. The City of Portland closed on Monday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Ashland, Oregon man died while swimming in the Pacific Ocean at Whaleshead Beach State Park on Oregon's south coast Saturday January 30th. Alexander Howell, 23, was swimming in the Pacific Ocean about 75 yards from shore with a friend, Alex Smith, age 22, from Medford, at about 2 p.m. when, according to witnesses, Howell indicated that he needed to go back to shore. Heavy surf and a strong rip current prevented the swimmers from making an easy return to shore, and the two got separated in the rough waters. Smith managed to get back to shore where two other friends were waiting but Howell was not able to get to shore and his friends were unable to initially find him. The group called 911 and searched the shoreline and found Howell unresponsive on the beach. The friends performed CPR until they were relieved by emergency responders. Howell was transported to Curry General Hospital in Gold Beach for advanced life support. He was declared deceased at the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Ashland, Oregon man died while swimming in the Pacific Ocean at Whaleshead Beach State Park on Oregon's south coast Saturday, January 30th. Alexander Howell, 23, was swimming in the Pacific Ocean about 75 yards from shore with a friend, Alex Smith, age 22, from Medford, at about 2 p.m. when, according to witnesses, Howell indicated that he needed to go back to shore. Heavy surf and a strong rip current prevented the swimmers from making an easy return to shore, and the two got separated in the rough waters. Smith managed to get back to shore where two other friends were waiting but Howell was not able to get to shore and his friends were unable to initially find him. The group called 911 and searched the shoreline and found Howell unresponsive on the beach. The friends performed CPR until they were relieved by emergency responders. Howell was transported to Curry General Hospital in Gold Beach for advanced life support. He was declared deceased at the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 42.3' steel-hulled crab fishing vessel Sarah Jo had a crew of 3. She was crossing the Coos bar around 4 pm local time, when the crankshaft broke, possibly as a result of rough conditions. After losing power, the boat then took a breaking wave on the bar, capsized, then sank. A USCG report shortly before the incident reported 10-12 foot steep breaking seas within the channel, and 8-10 foot steep seas just beyond the jetty. Conditions were worsening during rescue operations. Winds were south around 10 KT with 5 NM visibility. The bar was restricted to vessels below 40 feet. Note: The Sarah Jo was attempting to recover pots for the Eagle III, a crab fishing vessel that collided and broke up on the north jetty of Coos Bar on 01/19/2016, with the loss of 3 lives.||LOCATION: 43.355, -124.342: Within main channel of Coos Bar.||DEATHS: 1||MISSING PERSONS: 0||IMPACTS: All 3 crew were rescued from the water by the USCG and sent to the hospital. Raymundo Cardoso, 46, of Winchester Bay, later died from his injuries. Vessel sank in the channel and is most likely a complete loss. May also be a navigational obstruction.

Conditions were worsening during rescue operations. Winds were south around 10 KT with 5 NM visibility. The bar was restricted to vessels below 40 feet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large waves in Lincoln City caused the water to rise farther up the beach than normal, and knocked an elderly woman off her feet, breaking her leg. The wave also knocked down the injured woman's sister, but she did not sustain any injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced strong winds across the region. The winds took down trees and power lines resulting in traffic obstructions and property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced strong winds across the region. The winds took down trees and power lines resulting in traffic obstructions and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The BPA Tower at Mount Hebo measured a peak gust of 63 mph and the Tidewater RAWS measured a peak gust of 59 mph.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to some locations in southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calimus RAWS recorded a wind gust of 63 mph at 10/0100 PST and a gust to 61 mph at 10/0200 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of fronts brought high winds to some parts of southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Port Orford NOS/NWLON recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 60 mph recorded at 13/0648 PST, 13/0706 PST, and 13/0754 PST. The Long Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 13/0713 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a convective shower resulted in a large landslide that closed Oregon State Route 36 around 2 miles west of Triangle Lake for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A land slide consisting of 100 to 300 cubic yards of soil, rock, and large trees closed  Highway 36 at mile marker 25 for several days.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure gradients set up along the Blue Mountain Foothills, allowing for strong downslope winds mainly along the base of the foothills.  Some wind speeds in MPH are listed as followed: (75) 3 miles WSW of Kooskooskie, (64) just WNW of Cayuse, and (56) 5 miles SSW of Mission.

A cold front backed by a deep surface low resulted in strong winds across Northwest Oregon. Thunderstorms along the front produced damaging winds. Strong winds ahead of the front blew down a weak tree onto a moving vehicle, and resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 47 mph were measured at the Florence/Siuslaw Jetty in the morning with a peak gust of 57 mph. A peak gust of 59 mph was measured in Waldport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved along the Northern Oregon coast and produced strong winds across the region. The winds took down trees and power lines that obstructed traffic and resulted in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many weather reporting stations along the north Oregon coast measured sustained winds between 40 and 60 mph, and several of them had gusts between 73 and 79 mph. The winds downed several large trees, and Pacific Power estimated 22,000 customers along the coast were without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The BPA weather station at Mount Hebo measured a peak gust of 69 mph late in the morning. Other nearby weather stations measured sustained winds of 41 to 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved onshore on this date. As it moved inland strong gusts were recorded across southwest Oregon west of the Cascades.  Gusts verifying high wind warning criteria were only recorded at stations located on ridges, but many reports of damage and power outages were reported in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 61 mph was recorded at Cape Blanco at 13/1500 PST.  The NOS/NWLON at Port Orford recorded a gust to 64 mph at 13/1636 PST. An estimated 19251 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved onshore on this date. As it moved inland strong gusts were recorded across southwest Oregon west of the Cascades.  Gusts verifying high wind warning criteria were only recorded at stations located on ridges, but many reports of damage and power outages were reported in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2NNE Central Point reported a measured gust to 45 mph. A storage shed on the property was blown apart by the winds. Large branches down. A spotter 3NE Applegate reported 2 inch  branches coming off of trees.  Winds were estimated gusting to 45 mph. An estimated 998 customers were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved onshore on this date. As it moved inland strong gusts were recorded across southwest Oregon west of the Cascades.  Gusts verifying high wind warning criteria were only recorded at stations located on ridges, but many reports of damage and power outages were reported in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Onion Mountain RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 13/1545 PST, a gust to 59 mph at 13/1645 PST, and a gust to 64 mph at 13/1745 PST.  The Sexton Summit ASOS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 13/1751 PST. A spotter near Selma reported winds 45-55 mph , broken branches 2-3 inches in diameter, and power outages.  The Illinois Valley Fire Department reported numerous trees down from Cave Junction to Selma.  Some branches were 26-30 inches in diameter. An estimated 3619 customers were without power.

The Rail Fire, five miles west of Unity, Oregon, began on Sunday, July 31st. The cause is unknown and under investigation. The majority of the fuel in this area is mixed conifer with dead lodgepole. Portions of this area are part of the 2002 Monument fire. Over many weeks, in spite of suppression effort by the Wallowa-Whitman and Malheur National Forests, the fire grew to 41,716 acres. On September 7, 2016 it was 95% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cleveland Ridge Wildfire started at around 1600 PDT on 08/22/16.  It was caused by human activity. As of 30/0430 PST, the fire covered 530 acres and was 100% contained. 2.8 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cleveland Ridge Wildfire started at around 1600 PDT on 08/22/16. It was caused by human activity. As of 30/0430 PST, the fire covered 530 acres and was 100% contained. 2.8 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving front brought heavy rain to the Coast, resulting in flooding of local roads and a landslide in Tillamook County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain with accumulations of 2 to 4 inches along the Coast flooded Highway 6, and left standing water on Highway 101. A landslide occurred blocking Bayocean Road at milepost 2 as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave caused showers to continue across the area. One shower produced a brief funnel cloud east of Corvallis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oregon State University Meteorology Professor reported seeing a funnel cloud looking east of Corvallis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ridge of high pressure and offshore winds brought temperatures along the North Oregon Coast up into the mid 80s to mid 90s on August 25. Inland, temperatures on August 25-26 reached the upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the mid 80s to mid 90s lead to people seeking relief at local rivers, lakes, and beaches. One swimmer drowned at Siltcoos Lake south of Florence during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rail Fire started Sunday afternoon, July 31, approximately five miles west of Unity, Oregon. The cause is unknown and under investigation. The majority of the fuel in this area is mixed conifer with dead lodgepole. Portions of this area are part of the 2002 Monument fire. Over many weeks, in spite of suppression effort by the Wallowa-Whitman and Malheur National Forests, the fire grew to 41,716 acres. Fire became 95% contained on September 7th.  Rehab work continued until October 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ridge of high pressure and offshore winds brought temperatures along the North Oregon Coast up into the mid 80s to mid 90s on August 25. Inland, temperatures on August 25-26 reached the upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: News reported 8 runners were taken to the hospital with heat-related injuries during the Hood-to-Coast relay through Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Weigh Station Fire is burning near Meacham, Oregon. The fire is burning along Interstate 84 and has caused extended closure of the interstate. Although the fire is 95% contained and only about 700 acres, it did shut down Interstate 84 for an extensive period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bybee Creek Wildfire in Crater Lake National Park started at around 1330 PDT on July 28th.  The cause is unknown at this time, but it was likely due to human activities.  As of 0430 PST on 08/10/16, the fire 1072 acres and was 100% contained.  $4.7 million had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bybee Creek Wildfire in Crater Lake National Park started at around 1330 PDT on July 28th.  The cause is unknown at this time, but it was likely due to human activities.  As of 0430 PST on 08/10/16, the fire 1072 acres and was 100% contained.  $4.7 million had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Withers Wildfire, which was human caused, started at 1415 PDT on 08/17/16.  As of 25/0430 PST, the fire covered 3424 acres and was 90% contained.  1.6 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 80s to mid 90s lead to people seeking relief at local rivers. Two river drownings were reported in the Greater Portland Metro area during this heat event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Minam 4 Prescribe Burn experienced heavy winds that came in late Friday night and early Saturday morning causing the fire to go outside of the planned prescription area.|The fire is burning near Cougar Ridge in the Wallowa Fire Zone and is approximately 750 acres with the majority inside the wilderness boundary, 17 miles West of Joseph, Oregon. Regained control by end of October 2nd, beginning of October 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the Coast bringing strong winds to the North Oregon Coast, North Oregon Coast Range, and North and Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station at Mount Hebo, elevation approximately 3000 feet, recorded wind gusts peaking at 63 mph. In an adjacent zone, the broadcast media also reported a trees down along Chehalem Valley road.  Also, media outlets reported a tree fell over highway 47 near Beaver Creek Trailhead and a vehicle was stuck on the tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at Mary's Peak, elevation approximately 4000 feet,  measured sustained winds up to 59 mph with gusts peaking at 92 mph.  In an adjacent zone, the broadcast media also reported a trees down along Chehalem Valley road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the Coast bringing strong winds to the North Oregon Coast, North Oregon Coast Range, and North and Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Newport Airport reported max sustained wind at 40 mph with gusts peaking at 59 mph. Another weather station at Yachats reported gusts peaking at 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning on Oct 14th a fast moving squall developed and produced brief but strong wind gusts across a few locations in northeast Oregon. Wind gust reports varied from 60 mph in the Joseph area to 78 mph 4 miles NW of Cayuse.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced gusts up to 58 mph, recorded at the ASOS at the Astoria Airport.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rockhouse RAWS weather station reported a peak gust of 60 mph. On top of Mary's Peak, approximately 3,000 feet in the Coast Range, a peak gust of 102 mph was recorded. A trained spotter reported a 5 inch diameter Cherry Tree and an 80 year old apple tree toppled. Many 2 inch branches scattered around the area.  Oregon Department of Transportation reported that Highway 18 was closed at milepost 21 due to downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at Mt. Hebo recorded a peak gust of 59 mph. Another report in a nearby zone reported wind gusts of 61 mph along Chehalem road. The Oregon Department of Transportation reported downed trees across Highway 18 near Grande Ronde, closing the road at milepost 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds brought down several trees across the area, blocking roads and bringing down power poles and power lines. Wind also caused a grain silo to collapse near Newberg. In Marion County alone, 6600 people lost power as winds downed four feeder lines through Marion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning on Oct 14th a fast moving squall developed and produced brief but strong wind gusts across a few locations in northeast Oregon. Wind gust reports varied from 60 mph in the Joseph area to 78 mph 4 miles NW of Cayuse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter Estimated a 70 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.

A member of the public at Gold Beach reported a broken window due to wind as well as roofing material ripped from a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.

The Agrimet unit in Bandon reported a gust to 59 mph at 13/1715 PST.  The Long Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 63 mph at 13/1513 PST. A spotter near Bandon reported trees snapped or uprooted. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the Coast bringing strong winds to the North Oregon Coast, North Oregon Coast Range, and North and Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at Pacific City measured sustained winds of 56 mph with gusts peaking at 69 mph. Other weather stations along the North Oregon Coast recorded sustained winds of 45 to 55 mph with gusts to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the Coast bringing strong winds to the North Oregon Coast, North Oregon Coast Range, and North and Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of downed trees and power lines down, generally on the west side of the Greater Portland Metro Zone. Hillsboro ASOS weather station reported winds gusting up to 37 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the Coast bringing strong winds to the North Oregon Coast, North Oregon Coast Range, and North and Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McMinnville Airport ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 48 mph. Public reported multiple trees down south of Dayton, and a tree down west of Carlton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave generated showers and thunderstorms across the Portland Metro. Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Milwaukie near Fuller Road displaced 7 people from their home. Also, in Gladstone, heavy rain pushed waste water levels to the point that raw sewage leaked into the Clackamas River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rail Fire started Sunday afternoon, July 31, approximately ten miles southwest of Unity, Oregon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations at Clatsop Spit, Tierra Del Mar, and Pacific City all reported peak gusts of 61 mph. Also, in Manzanita, the emergency manager reported a tree fell on an unoccupied truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS/NWLON sensor at Port Orford recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 15/0719 PST and 15/1054 PST. The peak gust was 78 mph at 15/0942 PST.  The Long Prairie RAWS recorded gusts to 61 mph at 15/0813 PST and 15/0913 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced ping pong ball sized hail in Union County just northwest of Imbler on July 18th. Hail was measured at 1.25 inches in diameter. Penny sized (0.75) hail was also reported 6 miles SSW of Dale.

A thunderstorm produced ping pong ball sized hail in Union County just northwest of Imbler on July 18th. Hail was measured at 1.25 inches in diameter. Penny sized (0.75) hail was also reported 6 miles SSW of Dale.

A thunderstorm produced wind damage 8 miles north northwest of Imnaha and moved through Wallowa County on July 19th.  12 large trees were downed along Fence Line Rd. Also 1 inch hail was still seen 15 hours after the storm had passed, indicating hail was likely a lot larger during the storm. The hail destroyed a canvas tent and damaged a horse trailer and fire tower. Penny size (0.75) was also reported in Lostine on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 12 large trees downed.

Behind the front that moved through on October 13, unstable airmass generated strong convective showers and thunderstorms. A few of these thunderstorms produced tornadoes, strong winds, hail, and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey Team confirmed a EF-2 Tornado went through Manzanita, with estimated peak winds of 125-130 mph. Tornado started as a waterspout over the Pacific Ocean and came onshore near Treasure Cove Lane and continued northeast through Manzanita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station at Yachats reported a peak gust of 69 mph. Other stations reported peak gusts of 59 mph to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable airmass behind the front that moved through earlier in the day brought heavy showers into the Portland area. Heavy rain caused a small landslide in the Portland Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portland Bureau of Transportation reported a landslide blocking SE Malden Street and Southeast Sellwood Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain, large swell, and high tides brought minor tidal overflow flooding during high tides to the North Oregon Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KGW News shared a video of nuisance flooding in Nehalem during high tide around 12:47 pm PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through Baker County north of Baker City near Haines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported occasional one inch hail. Most of the hail was from an quarter of an inch to three quarters of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a brief EF0 tornado in rural Wallowa County on July 12th.  No other severe weather reports were received with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos of the tornado and information was submitted via email to us.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sexton Summit ASOS recorded gusts exceeding 57 mph nearly continuously between 15/1152 and 15/1223 PST. The peak gust was 70 mph at 15/1214 PST. The Oregon Department of Transportation reported that semi-trailers were being blown off the road on Interstate 5 at mileposts 65 and 69 near Sexton Summit. ODOT also reported a tree on Highway 46, which closed the road at milepost 2. Leaves and branches were reported on Interstate 5 near milepost 65.  A member of the public sent a picture of an uprooted tree in Grants Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought gusty winds to much of southwest and south central Oregon, but wind gusts reached warning levels only at high altitude stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calimus RAWS reported gusts to 58 mph at 24/0900 PST, 24/1000 PST, and 24/1100 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong front brought high winds to a number of locations around southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 63 mph continuously between 15/0614 and 15/1113 PST. The peak gust was 72 mph at 15/0913 PST. The Gold Beach ASOS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 15/0959 PST. A spotter 2SSE of Pistol River reported a gust to 70 mph at 15/0928 PST. A spotter 5NNW Brookings reported 3 oak trees 8 to 10 inches in diameter down on his property, along with power outages.  A spotter 1SE Brookings reported a gust to 67 mph at around 15/1000 PST. He also had a power outage that lasted around 45 minutes.  The Coastal Pilot newspaper reported that an 80 foot pine tree fell on a manufactured home along with downed power lines. A spotter in Gold Beach reported 3 trees down on his property.  He also had two awnings that were blown away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system passed north along the Coast bringing strong winds to Northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought many trees down across the area. Over 20,000 customers were without power. Downed trees blocked roads, fell on houses, and took down power lines. Wind or falling trees also snapped power poles in a few areas. Light rail service was disrupted due to power outages and downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bybee Creek Wildfire in Crater Lake National Park started at around 1330 PDT on July 28th.  The cause is unknown at this time, but it was likely due to human activities.  As of 0400 PST on 08/01/16, the fire covered 416 acres and was 10% contained.  $574,000 had been spent on firefighting efforts.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended storm brought heavy snow to portions of southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ODOT reported 12 inches of snow on Quartz Mountain Pass (5550 feet). A spotter 1W Crescent report 10 inches of snow in 14 hours ending at 14/1054 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds ahead of an approaching low pressure system brought temperatures down below freezing across the area ahead of the approaching precipitation. This lead to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area. While areas farther north saw more of a snow/sleet mix before a changeover to freezing rain then rain, areas in Lane County saw freezing rain for most of this event, causing power outages, damage to trees, and many car accidents around Eugene and Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Salem there were reports of 3 to 5 inches of snow. There were also numerous reports of vehicles that got stuck and huge traffic jams from the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds ahead of an approaching low pressure system brought temperatures down below freezing across the area ahead of the approaching precipitation. This lead to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area. While areas farther north saw more of a snow/sleet mix before a changeover to freezing rain then rain, areas in Lane County saw freezing rain for most of this event, causing power outages, damage to trees, and many car accidents around Eugene and Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of 2 to 4 inches in and around the Portland Metro area, which brought traffic to a halt. Over 500 vehicles were left abandoned on area roads once they turned icy. This storm essentially shut down the city for the next two days, since weather remained cold and snow could not melt. 1200 kids were stuck at schools in Beaverton until 8pm. Other school buses were still stuck with kids on them at 8:30 pm that night.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deer Creek at Roseburg rose above flood stage (10.0 feet) at 14/1630 PST, and reached moderate flood stage (13.0 feet) at 14/1930 PST.  The river crested at 13.05 feet at 14/2000 PST. It fell below moderate flood stage at 15/0100 PST and below flood stage at 15/0600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warmer low pressure system moved into to Northwest Oregon, bringing high winds along the North and Central Oregon Coast. Cold east winds through the Columbia River Gorge continued for the first part of the event, leading to light accumulations of snow and sleet in portions of far northwest Oregon and higher accumulations in the Columbia River Gorge and Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Megler Bridge weather observation station recorded a maximum sustained gust of 47 mph, while the Cape Disappointment station just to the north observed a max sustained wind of 43 mph with gusts to 53 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on the Yaquina Bridge reported a maximum sustained wind of 55 mph. Also, the Newport C-MAN station recorded a maximum sustained wind of 40 mph.

Cold east winds through the Columbia River Gorge continued for the first part of the event, leading to light accumulations of snow and sleet in portions of far northwest Oregon and higher accumulations in the Columbia River Gorge and Hood River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimate the Columbia Gorge had around 0.2 to 0.5 inch of ice accumulation as temperatures in the lower 30s with reports of snow and freezing rain in Hood River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds ahead of an approaching low pressure system brought temperatures down below freezing across the area ahead of the approaching precipitation. This lead to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area. While areas farther north saw more of a snow/sleet mix before a changeover to freezing rain then rain, areas in Lane County saw freezing rain for most of this event, causing power outages, damage to trees, and many car accidents around Eugene and Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marion Forks SNOTEL reported 15 inches of snow, and the Smith Ridge SNOTEL reported 13 inches of snow. An avalanche caused by heavy snow also closed US 20 across Santiam Pass.
